summary: Anthropic launched 'Dreaming' on May 6, 2026: a scheduled offline memory consolidation process that reviews past agent sessions, extracts cross-session patterns, and writes new memory entries for future agents. Harvey reported a 6× task-completion improvement after enabling it.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — AgentForge has per-run skill storage but no batch consolidation; Dreaming maps directly to synthesising meta-skills from groups of individual skill documents

summary: Open-source autonomous agent released Feb 2026 (160k+ stars) that converts successful task completions into reusable skill documents stored in SQLite, persists memory across sessions, and uses FTS5 for retrieval. Architecture includes SOUL.md, MEMORY.md, and per-skill markdown files.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — AgentSkillRepository infrastructure already exists; the specific gap (automated post-run skill extraction) is addressed by pending issues HID-17, HID-27

summary: Released Mar 7 2026 (21k+ stars, later 80k+). A tight autonomous experiment loop: agent edits training code, runs a 5-min job, measures improvement, commits or rolls back, repeats. Demonstrated 89-126 experiments per overnight session on H100.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — the commit-or-rollback pattern and cross-run metric tracking are relevant; HID-86 (automatic AIBlocked recovery with cross-run failure memory) covers the nearest gap

summary: April 2026 paper: agent that autonomously edits its own codebase to improve benchmark scores (17%→53% on SWE-Bench Verified). Unlike prior work, SICA acts as both initiator and recipient of self-improvement — no separate meta-agent.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — self-modifying agent loop is aspirational; HID-70 (credit-assigned prompt template evolution) covers the nearest existing gap

source: Speculative Actions: A Lossless Framework for Faster Agentic Systems (arxiv:2510.04371)
summary: ICLR 2026 paper: uses faster models to predict likely next actions and execute them in parallel with slower ground-truth executors; 55% next-action prediction accuracy, up to 20% latency reduction across gaming, e-commerce, and web environments.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — speculative execution is a compelling latency win; HID-47 (speculative next-stage pre-staging) already covers this direction

summary: 7B model trained with GRPO (RL) to route subtasks across GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro. Achieved 87.5% GPQA-Diamond vs GPT-5's 82.3%, at ~6× lower cost than full-frontier ensembles. Backbone of Sakana's Fugu commercial product.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — RL-trained routing is a step beyond heuristic routing; HID-85 (complexity-aware model cascade) already covers the model-routing gap with a heuristic approach

summary: Builds a dependency map between source files and tests before agent modification; agents know which tests to verify before committing. Reduces agent-caused regressions by 70%. Validates that blast-radius context injection materially improves coding agent reliability.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — no existing AgentForge issue covers pre-implementation import-graph dependency analysis; directly applicable to reduce AIBlocked states from cascade test failures

summary: ICLR 2025 benchmark by Shunyu Yao et al. showing task-specification quality is the dominant predictor of agent success rate; even frontier models succeed on <50% of under-specified tasks. Introduced pass^k reliability metric.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — no existing AgentForge issue addresses pre-flight quality scoring to prevent expensive runs on badly-specified issues; maps to HumanClarificationNeeded fast-path

summary: 2026 survey of agent observability patterns: OpenTelemetry typed spans, per-stage cost attribution, tail-based sampling, multi-dimensional cost rollup. Established production standard for agent tracing.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — multiple existing issues already cover this: HID-83 (per-stage metrics), HID-52 (execution telemetry), HID-20 (stage telemetry API), HID-45 (trace capture)

source: Fault-Tolerant AI Agent Patterns 2026 (Google ADK / Mindra / Getathenic)
summary: 2026 documentation of production-grade fault tolerance patterns for agent orchestration: exponential backoff with jitter, circuit breakers for LLM providers, layered fallback strategies, graceful degradation signaling.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — well covered by existing issues: HID-82, HID-66, HID-38, HID-33 (circuit breaker + retry), HID-57 (tiered escalation)

summary: SDK supporting episodic, semantic, and procedural memory for LLM agents. Procedural memory allows agents to rewrite their own system prompts based on feedback. Memory Managers extract, consolidate, and generalise from conversations.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — procedural memory and prompt rewriting covered by HID-70 (credit-assigned prompt template evolution); episodic skill memory by HID-17, HID-27

summary: Highest-scoring memory architecture on LoCoMo benchmark (0.7 judge score) as of early 2026. Multi-signal retrieval: semantic similarity, keyword matching, and entity matching fused in parallel.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — multi-view retrieval with temporal/causal indexing already covered by HID-49

source: Dynamic Model Routing 2026 (MindStudio / AI model routing surveys)
summary: 2026 survey: 37% of enterprises use 5+ models in production; intelligent routing achieves 40-60% cost reduction vs uniform frontier model deployment. Three-tier routing (fast/smart/power models) is the standard architecture.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — already covered by multiple issues: HID-85 (complexity-aware model cascade), HID-80 (complexity-based routing), HID-77 (task-complexity dynamic router)

summary: MIT-licensed orchestrator for parallel coding agents: each agent gets its own git worktree, branch, and PR; CI failures automatically routed back to the originating agent for autonomous fixing; agent-agnostic (Claude Code, Codex, Aider).
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — CI failure auto-remediation covered by HID-54; parallel worktrees already exist in AgentForge architecture
